title: Synthetic Flat Bands, Hierarchical Topology, and Phase-Fluctuation-Insensitive Quantized Transconductance in Josephson Junctions
authors: Subhadeep Chakraborty, Aabir Mukhopadhyay, Udit Khanna, Sourin Das
abstract: We uncover hierarchy of topological phases within the synthetic Brillouin zone of a three-terminal Josephson junction's (3-TJJ's) Bogoliubov-de Gennes spectrum. We demonstrate that the above-gap continuum realizes a Chern insulator phase with quantized monopole charges (\pm 1), while the subgap Andreev bound states (ABS) are characterized by a quantized dipolar invariant. By breaking time-reversal symmetry at the junction, we induce synthetic flat bands that suppress DC Josephson currents across the entire phase-bias space. Furthermore, under voltage bias, the junction exhibits a robust quantization of the time-averaged transconductance that is reminiscent of a quantized Hall conductance plateau owing to the flat band limit and its dipole phase. As a byproduct, the flat band produces a global "sweet plateau" of phase insensitivity, surpassing localized sweet spots of conventional superconducting qubits and enabling a robust architecture for symmetry-protected Andreev qubits.
summary: This paper explores the topological properties of three-terminal Josephson junctions, revealing a hierarchy of Chern and dipolar phases. It shows that at specific chiral points, synthetic flat bands emerge that suppress Josephson currents and create a 'sweet plateau' of phase insensitivity. This discovery provides a blueprint for a new class of Andreev qubits that are intrinsically protected against phase-bias noise.
main_problem: The paper addresses the challenge of mitigating dephasing and decoherence in superconducting qubits caused by fluctuations in superconducting phase biases.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that breaking time-reversal symmetry in a three-terminal Josephson junction creates synthetic flat bands that provide a 'sweet plateau' of phase insensitivity, enabling a robust architecture for symmetry-protected Andreev qubits.
method: The study uses an analytical approach involving the Bogoliubov-de Gennes (BdG) spectrum, Berry curvature integration for Chern numbers, and Wilson loops for dipole polarization, supplemented by numerical simulations of transconductance and density of states.
model_or_system: A three-terminal Josephson junction (3-TJJ) realized with semiconducting nanowires and proximity-induced s-wave superconductivity, modeled using a scattering matrix approach in a synthetic Brillouin zone defined by superconducting phase differences.
key_observables: Chern numbers, dipole polarization, time-averaged transconductance, Berry curvature, and Andreev bound state (ABS) energy spectrum.
important_parameters: The scattering parameter zeta, superconducting phase biases (phi2, phi3), chirality parameter delta_G, and the chiral points (zeta = 2pi/3, 4pi/3).
main_assumptions: The study assumes the short-junction limit for determining Andreev bound state energies.
figures_summary: Figures show Berry curvature contours, dipole polarization magnitude, and the identification of the dipolar phase; plots of time-averaged conductance and the gradient of ABS energy; and a comprehensive phase diagram mapping gap closings and topological transitions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the 3-TJJ system, analyzes the hierarchical topology (Chern vs. dipolar) of the BdG spectrum, demonstrates the emergence of synthetic flat bands at chiral points, explores the resulting quantized transconductance, and concludes with implications for robust qubit architectures.
why_interesting: It presents a novel paradigm for quantum information protection by moving from local 'sweet spots' to global 'sweet plateaus' using intrinsic Hamiltonian symmetries, which is highly relevant for the development of noise-resilient superconducting quantum architectures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic of a 3-TJJ realized with semiconducting nanowires and proximity-induced superconductivity. The indepen- dent phase biases ϕ2 and ϕ3 (relative to grounded ϕ1) act as syn- thetic quasimomenta, tunable via magnetic fluxes Φ2 = (ℏϕ2)/(2e) and Φ3 = (ℏϕ3)/(2e). A DC voltage bias V is applied to the second terminal.

FIG. 2. (a) The maximum and minimum gaps (in units of ∆) in the ABS spectrum. Green circles indicate ABS band touchings at zero energy, the blue circle indicates the ABS bands touching the contin- uum, and yellow squares indicate the chiral points of the S-matrix. The Chern number (C−) of the lowest occupied ABS band is plotted against ζ. (b) Berry curvature of the lowest occupied ABS band for four parameter values around the chiral points: (i) ζ = 0.65π, (ii) ζ = 0.7π, (iii) ζ = 1.33π, and (iv) ζ = 1.35π with zero Berry curvature contour separating positive and negative Berry curvature regions (c) The 1D dipole polarization p2 −is plotted against ϕ3 at and around the chiral points at...

FIG. 3. (a) Time averaged conductance ¯G23 is plotted against ϕ3 at and around chiral points. (b) Time-averaged conductance at chiral point ζ ≈0.67π is plotted in presence of random perturbation to the chiral scattering matrix (see Eq. 10). (c) The transconductance, total Chern number, and Chern number of the continuum and ABS are shown.

FIG. 4. Gradient of the ABS band energy with respect to supercon- ducting phases averaged over the entire s-BZ is plotted in (a). Shown in (b) is the fraction of the entire superconducting phase manifold over which the flatness is achieved with different flatness thresholds ϵ.
FIG. 5. ABS bands structure at different ζ values.
